LambdaMOO! Wow! Holy shit, I haven't seen anyone from there in a decade.
Also, I think Leah Bannon maybe introduced us one time when I stopped by for a late lunch.

Hi Everyone,

It was great to meet some of you today at the SF/Dynamicland meetup. While I've
been on Slack for a bit, I never posted to introductions so here it is.

I'm a software engineer professionally and over time I've grown increasingly
dissatisfied with the general state of affairs. It was all fun and games for a
while, but then I started watching Alan Kay videos :smile:. While digging into
historical ideas and banging my head against what seem to be a hard ceiling in
many of the tools and systems we use, I've arrived at the position that there is
a large space of possibilities that has been left unexplored while we continue
to incrementally hammer away at the entrenched systems. I believe big leaps can
only happen if many established fundamental ideas are changed or replaced. For
instance, we store and share *information* by encoding it in a
non-introspectable, very low level medium (aka files) that also loses its
connections to related information, history and identity. We then redesign these
features over and over in various ways in different contexts. Programs as text
files is another poor representation. We re-implement the same functionality and
schemas in different siloed processes and services. It seems our language for
interconnection and scaling up is really poor. Much of programming deals with
stuff that isn't really computing anything but just dealing with represenation
changes (e.g. parsing - doesn't change the information content). Worse, this
type of represenation shuffling code is tightly intermingled with the computing
code. We sit in front of powerful computers, and simulate them in our heads -
humans are simulating the general purpose simulator.

So what's an alternative? I don't know but perhaps creating a new
PL/DB/framework to fit in a PL/DB/framework shaped hole in the current mess may not
help. We need to explore ideas that are holisitic, ground up rethinking of what
computing is, how the space is partitioned into concepts and artifacts (note
files/filesystems, OS processes, databases, 'programs', compilers etc. are all
means to an end, serving the users achieve some of their goals - what are the
goals and what other ways can they be expressed?). How does something like this
'alternative substrate' or 'new material for computing' fit onto the existing
world? It doesn't, but my interest is more of a research exercise - how can it
be done, what would make it complete and self sustaining? If we have some good
ideas, we can prototype. Since this is broad and can go in all directions, I've
been writing to try and clear my thoughts and keep moving forward in some way.
If you're interested take a look at <https://shalabh.com/programmable-systems/>

attachments.text: Notes on the study of programmable computer systems.

text: Very possibly! Leah’s awesome.

Oops, I forgot to post my favourite computing paper: “Folk Computing: Revisiting Oral Tradition as
a Scaffold for Co-Present Communities”. In which a team from MIT take a couple of hundred Sega Dreamcast VMUs, spread them amongst school kids, and teach them how to make and share little programs. <https://llk.media.mit.edu/papers/archive/folk-computing.pdf>

<@UCSJ44X1S> i disagree with "stagnation". i think big periods of growth can be spawned with small revolutions:
- the launch of the iPhone and the growth of the gigantic App economy.
- the launch of the apple watch/ fitbit => potential for a new, automated, medical-grade economy

and theres' many many more bottoms up innovations that could really trigger massive growth: mesh networks, satellite broad-band internet, mobility.

even changes in taxation! if some big country in the world suddenly changes from our tiered, complex tax codes to something more revenue-based (lets say 5-10% per transaction), then there is a big potential for positive changes exists.

I'm well aware that a _simulation_ of an entire bacterium is generally beyond our computational abilities. To counterargue,
1. both bacteria and mitochondria vary a lot in size, and some are less than one micron in all dimensions.
2. a car is ~2 metres wides. The planet is ~20,000,000 metres from pole to pole. Across two dimensions that's (over) 14 orders of magnitude (510 trillion m^2). But individual cars are generally discernable in Google Maps.
3. I don't see what the problem is with the visualization. The person building the model knows the boundaries of each component and subcomponent (AFAIK the model cannot be produced automatically using, say, an electron microscope; experts in the detailed structure would have to make it, so they know the boundaries) so these boundaries can be easily highlighted for visualization. And with a GPU, local approximate simulations of parts of the cell should be possible.
4. I think lots of _software developers_ feel a low need for visualizations compared to the general population, but I don't feel that way. It's weird to me to hear someone imply that we need not "visualize" a physical object.

text: Yeah I think rebase-only could work. It would be necessary to keep the commit tree understandable. And having a single joining mechanism would simplify the model. When I show git to non-programmers, merging is always the biggest head-scratcher since people think of history as a linear thing.

Yeah I think rebase-only could work. It would be necessary to keep the commit tree understandable. And having a single joining mechanism would simplify the model. When I show git to non-programmers, merging is always the biggest head-scratcher since people think of history as a linear thing.

Yeah, maybe it's not a bad idea. But seems reasonable to me that nobody's tried it yet. There has to be a focused economic incentive to do something on the scale of Google maps. (And we'll have to agree to disagree that Google Maps is a reasonable proxy for the complexity of the project.)

I wasn't claiming that we shouldn't visualize physical objects. I was claiming that focusing on _just_ the physical is an intellectual disease. When we think of the world we mostly visualize maps. When we think of "Steve Jobs" we mostly visualize his body, which is why sharing the same restaurant or auditorium with it seems to become worthy of comment. But the geography of the world hides many flows that would be better off emphasized. Steve Jobs when he lived inhabited a far vaster phase space than just his body.

I think you should visualize a bacterium or mitochondrion out of building blocks of flows rather than atoms. That may end up being vastly more complex or less complex, depending on the details of the flows you choose. Neither should stop you.

text: Surely you would need to perform merges when you rebase? Resolve conflicts? Removing merges from the UI just means people have to manage them in their heads.
Surely you would need to perform merges when you rebase? Resolve conflicts? Removing merges from the UI just means people have to manage them in their heads.

Yeah, I mean, conceptually, two changes were made independently and then merged later. It's non-obvious how auto-merging is possible, but in the abstract, combining changes is an intuitive idea.

I admit to having avoided rebasing because
1. I don't understand well enough how it handles merge conflicts, and how to undo if I don't like the result
2. after rebase, the newest commits are "fake" - they contain files whose content (hash) never existed before, so theoretically unit tests could fail on commits that previously succeeded (new commits associated with the same commit messages)

text: <@U7C7B75R6> I definitely agree there’s value in teaching kids how computers work (at a high level) and what they’re capable of - a kind of automation literacy. In fact, I think it’s vital. So yes, it’s over-dramatic of me to say maybe we shouldn’t teach kids to code _at all_. Perhaps it’s more useful (but more boring) to teach in terms of benefits & dangers rather than “look, you can make anything!”

Rebasing and merging are not that different. The main difference is that a rebase doesn't keep a reference to the source commits but creates new ones. So conflicts work pretty much the same way, but there are some situations where it get's pretty bad when rebasing many commits with many conflicts.

Rebasing and merging are not that different. The main difference is that a rebase doesn't keep a reference to the source commits but creates new ones. So conflicts work pretty much the same way, but there are some situations where it get's pretty bad when rebasing many commits with many conflicts.
Isn't conflict resolution the biggest reason that merging is a head-scratcher to non-programmers?
Isn't conflict resolution the biggest reason that merging is a head-scratcher to non-programmers?

<@UD6EXQVM0> I wouldn't call the new commits "fake" though. They are different commits (ie snapshots), but with the same diffs. The old commits are still around even though no branch points to them anymore. And tests could also fail on a merge commit even though they passed on the merged commits. So that situation is very similar.

But yeah, being able to undo any operation like a rebase is definitely a must-have for any software that wants to make version control more accessible. Basically version-control of the version history.

<@UD6EXQVM0> I wouldn't call the new commits "fake" though. They are different commits (ie snapshots), but with the same diffs. The old commits are still around even though no branch points to them anymore. And tests could also fail on a merge commit even though they passed on the merged commits. So that situation is very similar.

But yeah, being able to undo any operation like a rebase is definitely a must-have for any software that wants to make version control more accessible. Basically version-control of the version history.

Yeah conflicts are a big head-scratcher. And they don't go away with rebasing and might even get worse. But I hope that they become simpler conceptually and less of a turn-off. But that will have to be tested big time.

My target is definitely small projects. I always feel like git is hopelessly overpowered for projects smaller than the Linux kernel. But that's why I wanna make it a facade for git so you can get the full power if you need.

text: To me, I would prefer to treat rebasing as a view rather than a change to the underlying commits.  I mean the ground truth is that the nest of messy commits and a rebase a sort of homomorphism that untangles them.

text: Btw I found a reasonable-looking estimate of the number of atoms in a bacterium of E. coli weighing 10^-12 g: 93 billion, of which 61% are hydrogen atoms (and about 2/3 are water). It's unclear if this is a typical bacterial size but it does appear to be a typical E. coli size based on this page <http://book.bionumbers.org/how-big-is-an-e-coli-cell-and-what-is-its-mass/>

Yeah, I always like seeing animations like that, I just wish I could zoom in and out and pan - zoom out to see things in context, zoom in to get a sense of how molecular machines work, lower the playback speed...
